titleInfo.title: On the Investigation of Seng’an’s Engraved Sutras

mods.abstract: Following Kiriya2003a, Lai Fei presents an even more detailed, hypothetical biography of Seng’an Daoyi. P. 99 contains his defense for reading the name of the monk as "Seng’an Daoyi". He follows Zhang Weiran 張偉然 and Xu Hongguo. He first discusses inscription sites and their associated donor names, then the chronology of the sites visited by Seng’an (p. 115-119): Point of departure: Mount Hongding, region of Chongfan Monastery, Mount Yi, Mount Tao, Mount Shuiniu, Yanzhou. P. 115: Lai Fei estimates an age of more than 40 years when visiting Mount Hongding, and more than 60 years when writing Mount Ge. Travelling to Ye in the west: Da kong wang fo and 無垢佛 、 彌勒佛、獅子佛、明炎佛 at Northen Mount Xiangtang (Lai Fei rejects Seng’an as writer of Tang Yong stele). Lai Fei thinks that Seng’an did not take part in Tang Yong’s project, but only wrote the Buddhanames in 572. From 572-574, he then wrote sutra passages and Da kong wang fo at Southern Mount Xiangtang. Return to the east: In 575, he came back with Lady Zhao, whom he had met at the capital when being invited by Tang Yong, and created Mount Jian. Fourth phase of propagation of the Dharma with support by members of the upper social strata: Mount Tai in 576, which remained unfinished because of persecution under Zhou Wudi. Lai Fei believes that this persecution was not as severe as the histories want us make believe. In 579, Seng’an wrote Mount Tie, and in 580 Mount Ge. When Mount Gang was created, he had probably already died. p. 119-120: These four phases can be arranged in two large phases, with Mount Jian as turning point, i.e., before and after 575. The sites are different in size, contents (Buddhanames and sutra passages), collaborators, calligraphic style, and intention (禪宋 and 護法) p. 120-124 table showing inscriptions by Seng’an at the Shandong sites (and steles) and at Northern and Southern Mount Xiangtang, passages carved, Seng’an’s signatures and his alleged collaborators. p. 124-131: Explanation of the distinctions concerning size, contents, collaborators, calligraphic style, and intention within the two phases, before and after 575.
